metadata.seed: Okay, so I'm going to class. I'm fine. I sit down to take the test. I'm fine. The test is in front of me. Question one. I'm not going to get that answer.

patient.query: Okay, so I'm going to class. I'm fine. I sit down to take the test. I'm fine. The test is in front of me. Question one. I'm not going to get that answer.

triggers: sitting down to take the test
automatic_thoughts: I'm not going to get that answer

entailment: On examination - state of mind normal (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling pretty calm overall, yet you’re also doubting your ability to answer the first question. What evidence do you have that you won’t get that answer right? How might you view this situation if you considered a different possibility?

patient.query: I guess I do feel a bit calmer, but the doubt just creeps in, you know? It’s like this voice that says I’m not smart enough or I'll mess it up. I just keep thinking about the times I didn't do well before, and it kind of overshadows any good moments. It's frustrating because I want to believe I could do better, but it's hard to shake that feeling.

llm_response.response: It sounds like those doubts are really pulling you down, even when you feel a bit calmer. What evidence do you have that you’re not smart enough, and can you think of any moments that contradict that idea? Exploring those details might help you see a more balanced view of your abilities.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: automatic thoughts about not being smart enough
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: I mean, I know I’ve done okay in some classes, but then I think about the tests I barely passed or the times I felt lost during lectures. It's like I can remember every mistake more clearly than the things I did right. I guess I just hold onto those failures, and they make it hard to see anything good. It’s really frustrating because I want to focus on the positives, but the negatives feel too loud to ignore.

triggers: times I felt lost during lectures
automatic_thoughts: I remember every mistake more clearly
the negatives feel too loud to ignore

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught in a loop where the mistakes feel louder than the successes. What evidence do you have that the failures outweigh the positives? Can you recall moments when you felt confident or achieved something? How might you view those successes alongside the challenges you’ve faced?

patient.query: Yeah, I get that. It’s just hard to pull those moments of confidence to the front of my mind. I mean, there have been times when I’ve worked really hard and got a good grade or felt like I understood something, but they seem so small compared to the looming stress of what’s coming next. I guess I get so overwhelmed by the pressure and the fear of failing again that I just block out the good stuff. It’s like I’m afraid to hope, you know?

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure you’re feeling is so intense that the good moments feel almost invisible. When the fear of failing looms large, it’s understandable that you might push those successes aside as a way to protect yourself. Acknowledging how overwhelming that can be is an important first step.

patient.query: Yeah, it really does feel overwhelming sometimes. I think I just keep waiting for that moment when everything clicks, but then I end up feeling more anxious. It’s like I’m on this treadmill, running to keep up but never getting anywhere. And I wonder if I’ll always feel like this, stuck in the cycle of worry and doubt. It’s exhausting, honestly.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure, feeling like you’re constantly running but not making progress. That can be exhausting and make you question whether this will ever change. It’s understandable to feel stuck in that cycle of worry and doubt.

patient.query: Yeah, it really is tiring. It feels like I’m always waiting for things to get easier, but then there’s just more to do, more to prove. I keep thinking if I could just get it right this time, maybe I wouldn’t feel so trapped. But then I start to doubt if I even have it in me. It’s like I'm caught in this loop where nothing feels good enough.

triggers: waiting for things to get easier
automatic_thoughts: if I could just get it right this time
I doubt if I even have it in me

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy sense of exhaustion and frustration, and that feeling of being stuck in a never‑ending cycle. You’re noticing how each new task or expectation seems to add to that sense of being trapped. It’s understandable that you’d feel doubtful and that nothing seems good enough right now.

patient.query: Yeah, that really resonates. I feel like I’m just trying to keep up, but my mind keeps replaying the doubts. It’s like I can see the finish line, but every time I get close, something else pops up. I know I should be able to celebrate the little wins, but I just can’t shake that feeling that it won’t be enough. It's disheartening, and I don't know how to pull myself out of that space.

automatic_thoughts: my mind keeps replaying the doubts

neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught in a cycle of doubt, even when you’re near a goal. What evidence do you have that your achievements aren’t enough? Can you think of a time when a small win felt meaningful to you?

patient.query: I guess I just look at the bigger picture and feel like I’m always way behind. I mean, even when I do something well, there's always that nagging thought that I could have done better or that it won’t matter in the long run. There was that time I got a decent grade on a paper, and for a moment it felt good. But then I started thinking about all the other assignments I still had to tackle, and it just faded into the background. It’s like I

triggers: getting a decent grade on a paper
automatic_thoughts: It won't matter in the long run

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure on yourself, especially when you think about what’s still ahead. When you get a decent grade, what evidence do you have that it doesn’t matter or that you’re still behind? Maybe we can explore ways to recognize the value of the work you’ve already done and how it fits into the bigger picture.

patient.query: Yeah, it feels like there's always more to do, and I can't help but look at what’s left instead of what I’ve accomplished. I guess I just compare myself to others and feel like I'm lagging behind, even if that doesn’t really reflect reality. Sometimes I think, “What if they had to deal with what I did?” But then I just end up feeling like an imposter, like I don’t belong. It’s hard to see those accomplishments as enough when I feel that

automatic_thoughts: I can't help but look at what’s left
what if they had to deal with what I did?

neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how it feels like you’re constantly chasing a moving target. What evidence do you have that others are always ahead of you? How might you view your own achievements if you looked at them from a different angle?

patient.query: I mean, I guess I don’t really have solid evidence—it’s more of a feeling that I get when I see classmates doing well or talking about their successes. It’s like I can’t help but compare myself to them, and it makes me feel small. If I tried to look at my own achievements differently, maybe I could focus on how far I’ve come instead of how far I still have to go? But it’s tough to shift that perspective; it feels like I’ve spent so

automatic_thoughts: I can't help but compare myself to them

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern of comparison that leaves you feeling small. What evidence do you have that supports the idea you’re falling behind, and what evidence might show you’ve made progress? How might you view your achievements if you considered them from a different perspective?
